examples.title: Basic Command Line Argument Printer
examples.description: Demonstrates how to access and print all command line arguments passed to a program
examples.code:
PROGRAM PrintArguments
BEGIN
    PRINT "Number of arguments: " + argc
    PRINT "Arguments passed:"
    
    FOR i FROM 0 TO argc-1 DO
        PRINT "argv[" + i + "] = " + argv[i]
    END FOR
    
    RETURN 0
END
examples.explanation: This program shows how argc and argv work. It prints the total count and iterates through the argv array to display each argument. Note that argv[0] is the program name itself.

examples.title: C++ Program to Add Two Numbers from Command Line
examples.description: Accepts two integers as command line arguments and displays their sum
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates proper argument validation and conversion. It checks argc, converts strings using stoi() with try-catch for error handling, and provides usage message on error.

examples.title: C++ Program with Options Parsing
examples.description: Demonstrates parsing of command line options/flags using getopt
examples.explanation: This demonstrates getopt() for parsing options. Options with colons require arguments (-o:), others are flags (-v). optind tracks non-option arguments. Handles error cases properly.

examples.title: File Word Counter with Optional Verbose Flag
examples.description: Complete utility that counts lines, words, and characters in a file with optional verbose output
examples.explanation: This complete program demonstrates manual option parsing, file handling, error checking, verbose mode implementation, and proper exit codes. Shows real-world command line utility patterns.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is file size
